text: The video shows an injury to the small intestine caused by the trocar, with visible bleeding on the mesentery and intestine. A small dissection of the mesentery is performed, revealing a perforation in the small intestine.
The surgeon visually examines both sides of the perforated small intestine, assessing the extent of the injury and any associated bleeding.
The video shows the exit entry site of the sharp trocar on one side of the small intestine, while the opposite side displays the entry site of the perforation.
The video shows a wider entry wound and a smaller exit wound on the small intestine caused by a pyramidal-shaped trocar, highlighting the difference in wound size.
The surgeon begins intracorporeal suturing on the small intestine, using surgical instruments to stitch the perforated area caused by the trocar.
The surgeon carefully sutures the small bowel, ensuring that the opposite wall of the lumen is not included in the suture bite.
The surgeon performs seromuscular closure on the small bowel, approximating the edges of the tissue to facilitate healing.
The surgeon performs an anterior angio repair using a surgeon's knot, carefully tying the suture to approximate the edges of the tissue.
The surgeon repairs the posterior wall of the bowel, ensuring precise alignment and closure of the tissue edges.

The surgeon completes the entry into the abdominal cavity, ensuring careful handling to avoid injury. The anterior wall repair is visible, followed by the posterior wall repair of the same layer. Both repairs involve through-and-through bites for complete approximation of the tissue.
The surgeon applies fibrin glue to the sutured area of the bowel to seal any potential injury, ensuring the integrity of the repair.
The surgeon ties a knot, known as the surgeon's knot, securing the suture in place during the small bowel resection procedure.
The surgeon places two sutures on the posterior wall and three bites on the anterior wall of the bowel during the repair process.
The surgeon performs intracorporeal suturing to control a visible complication in the surgical field.
